definition:
كتاب الرّاء

رحم - رحي

ورجل مُرْحِل: كثير الرّواحِل. ويقولون في القَذْف: "يا ابنَ مُلْقَى أرحُلِ الرُّكْبان"، يشيرون به إلى أمْرٍ قبيح.

(رحم) الراء والحاء والميم أصلٌ واحدٌ يدلُّ على الرّقّة والعطف والرأفة. يقال من ذلك رَحِمَه يَرْحَمُه، إذا رَقَّ لـه وتعطَّفَ عليه. والرُّحُْم والمَرْحَمَة والرَّحَْمة بمعنىً. والرَّحِم: عَلاقة القرابة، ثم سمِّيت رَحِمُ الأنثى رَحِماً من هذا، لأنّ منها ما يكون ما يُرْحَمُ وَيُرَقّ لـه مِن ولد. ويقال شاةٌ رَحُومٌ ، إذا اشتكَتْ رحِمَها بعد النِّتاج؛ وقد رَحُمَتْ رَحَامَة، ورُحِمَت رَحْما . وقال الأصمعيّ: كان أبو عمرو بن العلاء يُنشد بيتَ زُهير:

مِن سيِّئ العَثَرات اللهُ والرُّحُمُ

ومَن ضرِيبته التّقوَى ويَعصِمُه

قال: ولم أسمَعْ هذا الحرفَ إلاّ في هذا البيت. وكان يقرأ: ] وَأَقْرَبَ رُحُماً [ [الكهف 81]، وكأن أبا عمروٍ ذهب إلى أنّ الرُّحُمَ الرَّحْمة. ويقال إنّ مكّة كانت تسمَّى أمَّ رُِحْم .
definition_english:
This entry, presented by Ibn Faris, outlines the foundational meaning of the Arabic root R-Ḥ-M (ر ح م). It is preceded by a short, distinct section that appears to discuss terms related to the root R-Ḥ-L (ر ح ل), likely due to a shared letter or proximity in lexicographical arrangement.

---

**Preliminary Notes on Related Terms (from R-Ḥ-L)**

*   A **_murḥil_** (مُرْحِل) man is described as one who possesses many riding camels (*rawāḥil*).
*   In the context of severe insult or slander (**_qadhf_**), the phrase "Oh son of where the riders' saddles are cast" is uttered. This expression points to a highly disgraceful matter, implicitly denouncing the recipient's lineage with an extreme vulgarity related to a place where travelers would dismount and engage in illicit acts.

---

### Core Semantic Principles

The letters Rāʾ (ر), Ḥāʾ (ح), and Mīm (م) constitute a single fundamental origin (**_aṣl wāḥid_**) that primarily signifies **tenderness (_riqqa_)**, **affection (_ʿaṭf_)**, and **compassion (_raʾfa_)**.

### Derivations & Evidence

From this core principle, Ibn Faris delineates various linguistic applications and illustrative examples:

*   **Verbal Derivations:**
    *   The verb *raḥimahu yarḥamuhu* (رَحِمَه يَرْحَمُه) is used to express that someone "had mercy upon him" or "showed him compassion." This occurs when one feels tenderness and affection towards another.

*   **Nominal Synonyms:**
    *   The nouns *al-ruḥm* (الرُّحُْم), *al-marḥama* (المَرْحَمَة), and *al-raḥma* (الرَّحَْمة) are all considered synonymous, conveying the core meaning of mercy, compassion, or tenderness.

*   **The Concept of _al-Raḥim_ (Womb/Kinship):**
    *   **_Al-raḥim_** (الرَّحِم) fundamentally denotes the bond of kinship.
    *   From this, the female uterus or womb was subsequently named **_raḥim_**. This naming is derived from the original meaning because the womb is the source from which a child is born, and children are naturally objects of mercy, tenderness, and compassion.

*   **Specific Usage for Animals:**
    *   The phrase *shāh raḥūm* (شاةٌ رَحُومٌ) is used to describe an ewe that experiences pain or ailment in her womb after lambing.
    *   This state is further described by the verbal forms *raḥumat raḥāma* (رَحُمَتْ رَحَامَة) and *ruḥimat raḥman* (رُحِمَت رَحْما), indicating the ewe's suffering or being in a condition that evokes compassion.

*   **Poetic and Quranic Evidence with Lexicographical Commentary:**
    *   **Zuhayr's Poetry:** Al-Aṣmaʿī recounted that Abū ʿAmr ibn al-ʿAlāʾ, a renowned grammarian and reciter, used to recite a verse by Zuhayr:
        > "God and *al-ruḥum* (divine compassion/kinship) save him from grievous stumbling,
        > And whose nature is piety and it protects him."
        Al-Aṣmaʿī noted that he had encountered the word *al-ruḥum* (الرُّحُم) in this specific form only in this particular verse. Ibn Faris includes this to illustrate a classical poetic usage of *al-ruḥum* as a powerful protective force, associated with God and interpreted as mercy.
    *   **Quranic Recitation:** Abū ʿAmr ibn al-ʿAlāʾ also famously recited the Quranic verse from Sūrat al-Kahf (18:81) as: "and closer in *ruḥumā*" (وَأَقْرَبَ رُحُماً). The standard reading is *ruḥman*. Abū ʿAmr's unique reading here, and his general interpretation, suggest that he understood *al-ruḥum* to mean *al-raḥma* (mercy). This reinforces the root's core meaning of compassion and suggests that God would replace the problematic boy with one who is "closer in mercy" or more compassionate to his parents.
    *   **Mecca's Historical Name:** It is also mentioned that Mecca was once known as *Umm Riḥm* (أمّ رُِحْم), which translates to "Mother of Mercy" or "Mother of Kinship." This appellation further connects the city to the root's foundational meanings of compassion, familial bonds, and perhaps a place where mercy is sought or found.
